TextENGNew: ...What are you doing?
It's quite unusual for you to start playing on your own.
You were a being who could only nod in response to my questions... When did you develop a will of your own...?
...I'm happy.
That I finally have a little sister -- or perhaps a brother?@k Now that you're here.
changeReason: New adds 'little' sister and changes 'whichever you are' to 'now that you're here,' altering the nuance of the sibling relationship.

TextENGNew: Once, I too was an existence of nothingness, just like you.
Yet in time, I came to possess my own sense of self, my own thoughts, and eventually, a form to call my own.
So I don't find it strange at all that you've developed a will of your own.

Welcome to this world that stretches infinitely yet remains forever sealed and narrow, my dear sister.@k You may turn out to be my brother, but since I can't tell your sex, I'll call you my sister for now.
This is a world of endless eternity and infinity.
But that doesn't mean it's boundlessly vast.
Let me think...@k Perhaps the best way to describe it would be a ring with a very small radius.
So long as you keep going around and around the ring, it's an eternal world without end...
But that by no means implies it possesses infinite expanse.
Yes -- in short, this is an eternally stagnant world.
changeReason: Original translates 閉塞 as 'sealed'; new uses 'stagnant,' which better captures the JPN nuance of being trapped in a dead-end state.

TextENGNew: The future ought to branch into countless choices, spreading out infinitely...@k ...yet somehow, our world is always sealed shut by an ending called 'death.'
If I had to liken it to something, I suppose you could call it a labyrinth of fate.
When you spend forever going around and around the same places... it gradually becomes painful and tedious, until eventually something like me emerges.
...And if that continues even further, producing even you, then perhaps this world will eventually be filled with your sisters too.
Once our colony is built here and we lose interest in this narrow labyrinth world, that will be the moment we 'Rika Furudes' vanish for good.
...Rika still seems to cling to this narrow world.@k But I lost interest long ago.
That's why I'm truly glad you were born -- someone for me to talk to.
So, what have you been doing this whole time?
Playing with building blocks made of memory Fragments?
changeReason: Original says 'collecting Fragments' but JPN 積み木遊び means 'playing with building blocks made of memory fragments'; new correctly describes the activity.

TextENGNew: There are only five or six of them right now.
But even so, you might be able to have a bit of fun with them.
There used to be many, many more.
...But little by little, they dissolved into the darkness and vanished.
The only ones that remain clearly are the ones you see there.
Ah, that's not how you use it.
Here -- hold it up like this, and take a look.
Each one of these is a life of Rika Furude -- and its end.
That one is... Oh... how nostalgic.
That's the story of the world where Keiichi was ensnared.
changeReason: Original renders 囚われた as 'cursed'; new correctly translates as 'ensnared,' better matching the meaning of being trapped by darkness.

TextENGNew: I think of these Fragments as something like books, so I've titled that one the 'Onikakushi' chapter.
Do you remember what happened in the 'Onikakushi' chapter?
...Good.@k That makes things simpler.
In the world of 'Onikakushi,' Keiichi Maebara was consumed by darkness.
A tragic tale in which he could not accept the concern his friends offered, and in the end beat them to death without ever recognizing their kindness.
And after that, we were killed as well -- as if on schedule.
Entirely unrelated to his story.
That Fragment over there is the 'Watanagashi' chapter.
The one ensnared in that world was Shion Sonozaki.
changeReason: Original renders 囚われた as 'cursed'; new correctly translates as 'ensnared,' consistent with the metaphor of being trapped.

TextENGNew: Her vengeance for a lover who vanished in 1982 spiraled out of control, and she ended up killing many people.
changeReason: New adds 'spiraled out of control' for 暴走 (rampage/loss of control) which is absent from the original, and changes sentence structure to better match JPN.

TextENGNew: Rika tried so hard in that one.
That's why, in the 'Meakashi' chapter -- when the same scenario played out again -- she chose to take her own life rather than face the torture.
That Fragment over there is a world I call the 'Tatarigoroshi' chapter.
...Satoko's uncle returned and took her away, locking her inside.
In a sense, that was the unluckiest world of all.
changeReason: Original says 'worst world' but JPN 運のない means 'unlucky'; new correctly translates as 'unluckiest world,' preserving the notion of bad luck rather than quality.

TextENGNew: A dead end where nothing Rika could do would make any difference.
Honestly, when the uncle comes back, it's probably best to just lament the bad luck and give up on that world.
changeReason: Original attributes action to Rika specifically; new more accurately renders the JPN advisory tone of suggesting anyone should give up when the uncle returns.

TextENGNew: Fortunately, the odds of his coming back aren't all that high.
That one is a world I call the 'Tsumihoroboshi' chapter.
It's the world Rika was in until just a moment ago.
The one ensnared in that world was Rena Ryuugu.
The siege she laid -- taking an entire class hostage -- was quite the spectacle.
changeReason: Original says 'take over the whole school'; new correctly specifies 'taking an entire class hostage' for JPN クラス中を人質, which refers to the class, not the whole school.

TextENGNew: What truly surprised me was that Keiichi Maebara possessed memories of other worlds, just as we do.
Such a vivid case was certainly rare, but in truth, everyone carries fragments of such memories in their everyday lives.
An imagined world of 'what if' -- wondering how things might have turned out if you had done something differently.
The sense of deja vu when something you should be experiencing for the first time feels as though you've already lived through it.
Everyone retains fragmentary memories of alternate worlds, but they lack the ability to consciously perceive them.
changeReason: Original vaguely says memories are 'not always that clear'; new correctly states people 'lack the ability to consciously perceive them,' reflecting JPN 知覚することができない.

TextENGNew: Naturally.
So the very fact that you can see multiple Fragments of memory -- those building blocks you've been playing with -- means you are a truly extraordinary being.
changeReason: Original truncates to 'You are special enough to see Fragments'; new preserves the full JPN meaning about seeing MULTIPLE fragments being the special part.

TextENGNew: Realizing how special that makes you... may be difficult for you.
After all, you were born into a world where seeing multiple Fragments is simply the natural state of things.
I was born not in this world, but in the singular, ordinary one.
So the idea of playing with Fragments of memory like building blocks was something I only learned quite recently.
What you did casually, just fidgeting out of idle curiosity, took me an eternity to figure out.
...I'm sorry.
No matter how much I talk about myself, you probably can't understand, and you probably aren't interested.
Hmm?@k What is it?@k What has you so puzzled?
...What's that?@k You're wondering why such wildly different incidents occur on the same stage?
Hee hee.@k ...Yes, that is a very interesting question.
Why do you think that is?
Let's think about it together.
After all, thinking is the first step of any game.
For instance, among Rika's friends, the one with the greatest presence is Keiichi Maebara.
changeReason: Original calls Keiichi 'the main character'; JPN 存在感の大きい means 'one with the greatest presence,' which new correctly uses.

TextENGNew: In the 'Onikakushi' chapter, he was consumed by paranoid suspicion and beat Rena Ryuugu and Mion Sonozaki to death.
But if that were inevitable, then Keiichi Maebara would have to beat Rena and Mion to death in every world.
In other words, Keiichi Maebara's violence in 'Onikakushi' was not an inevitability defined by who he is.
Inevitability, you see... is something like this.
changeReason: Original 'the only thing that's inevitable' doesn't match JPN; new correctly renders as 'inevitability is something like this' as an introduction to the concept.

TextENGNew: It refers to the role of the piece called Keiichi Maebara.

If Keiichi Maebara were someone inherently prone to extreme suspicion, someone who habitually distorted the world around him,@k then he would have taken the same actions in every world, not just in 'Onikakushi.'
changeReason: Original significantly truncates the JPN, losing the description of Keiichi as someone who 'habitually distorted the world'; new preserves this characterization.

TextENGNew: But that didn't happen.
It does seem true that the piece called Keiichi Maebara is impulsive and prone to jumping to conclusions.
changeReason: Original says 'passionate and easily convinced of anything'; new more precisely translates 直情的 as 'impulsive' and 思い込みが激しい as 'prone to jumping to conclusions.'

TextENGNew: However, the kind of rampage he exhibited in 'Onikakushi' is clearly an unnatural irregularity when viewed against his typical role.
changeReason: Original says behavior is simply 'rare'; new correctly translates as 'clearly an unnatural irregularity when viewed against his true role,' matching JPN 明らかに不自然なイレギュラー.

TextENGNew: In other words, even something that appears inevitable within a single world may prove to be nothing of the sort when you overlay and compare multiple worlds.
Conversely, events that are truly destined to occur will repeat unchanged no matter how many worlds you traverse.
That is what true inevitability looks like.
This is a very important concept.
When you overlay the Fragments of multiple worlds, whatever facts remain constant across all of them are closest to the truth.@k Do you understand?
As I said before, facts that differ from world to world -- even if they seem inevitable within any single one -- are nothing but capricious coincidence when viewed across many.
...The club activities Mion Sonozaki organizes are a good example.
If Mion chose them with some strong, deliberate purpose, the same activity would be played in every world.
But in reality, the activity is different in nearly every world.
This tells us that when it comes to choosing what game to play, Mion Sonozaki decides on a whim -- randomly.
This is something we can discern effortlessly, since we can observe multiple worlds.
But humans with memories of only a single world cannot tell whether a given activity was chosen on a 'whim' or 'deliberately planned with strong intent.'
In other words, what's impossible for single-world humans to deduce about 'the inner workings of Mion Sonozaki's mind,' we can deduce with ease.
The specific activity may be random, but in nearly every world, Mion reliably holds club activities after school.
This means that on the single point of holding club activities, Mion Sonozaki's 'strong will' is clearly at work.
So what we alone can see is that Mion Sonozaki 'absolutely wants to play together after school,'@k but 'picks whatever game happens to come to mind.'
changeReason: Original truncates to 'she doesn't know what to play'; new correctly renders the full JPN about picking games on a whim, preserving the analytical framework.

TextENGNew: You see?
Even a simple game of building blocks reveals quite a bit about what Mion Sonozaki thinks of her club, doesn't it?
changeReason: Original says 'we can read Mion's mind'; new more accurately renders the JPN nuance of 案外わかる (surprisingly understandable) rather than full mind-reading.

TextENGNew: With just that alone, part of the role of the piece called Mion Sonozaki comes into view.
Now, let me return to Keiichi Maebara.

For humans who possess only a single world's memory, the 'Onikakushi' chapter is nothing more than a story in which Keiichi suffered inexplicable derangement, fell into paranoid suspicion, and bludgeoned his friends to death.
No matter how deeply they investigate, they can learn nothing beyond that.
But we can overlay many Fragments and discover truths that they could never perceive.
And what we discover is that his killing of his friends was not inevitable.
I said that at the very beginning.
Among the five or six Fragments in your hands, Keiichi Maebara murders his friends in only one.
This means -- to put it crudely -- that Keiichi triggering the tragedy carries a probability of less than twenty percent. You might even call it a mere 'fluke.'
In other words, across multiple worlds, Keiichi Maebara causing the tragedy is not a particularly significant factor.
Now, can you find a common thread here...?
Oh my, you've noticed already?
...Impressive. You truly were born for this world.
You learn the knack for this building-block game quickly.
It took me quite a while to notice.
...Being born in the ordinary world, I could only see things from a single world's perspective.
...I was fixated on the point of 'Keiichi Maebara,' and it took me a long time to notice.
Yes, exactly.
At the very least, 'Onikakushi,' 'Watanagashi,' 'Meakashi,' and 'Tsumihoroboshi' share one clear commonality.
In each one, an unspecified individual is seized by paranoid suspicion and driven to violence.
In each world,
Keiichi Maebara,
or Shion Sonozaki,
or Rena Ryuugu -- one of them spirals deeper into delusion until they're driven to violence.
Keiichi Maebara, Shion Sonozaki, and Rena Ryuugu are all close acquaintances, but they are strangers to one another in terms of birth and upbringing -- entirely different people.
changeReason: Original says they are 'my close friends'; new correctly uses neutral 'close acquaintances' (this is narrated by a meta-being, not a friend), and specifies they are 'strangers to one another.'

TextENGNew: ...Beyond the fact that they all appear on the stage called Hinamizawa, no common thread can be found.
Yes -- I believe I said this myself just earlier.
When you overlay and peer through the Fragments of multiple worlds,
truth lies in the phenomena that recur consistently, while erratic phenomena hold little significance.
In other words, the individual 'culprits' -- who differ from world to world -- are not especially important in themselves.
To go further, even the specific 'acts of violence' that constitute the incidents hold no particular meaning.
Keiichi Maebara bludgeoned two friends to death.
Shion Sonozaki massacred the leading members of the Three Families,@k and Rena Ryuugu occupied the school.
All completely different.@k No common thread whatsoever.
What is far, far more important is this: even though the culprit and the crime are random,@k the 'process' of descending into paranoid suspicion before resorting to violence is perfectly consistent.
Despite the piece that becomes the culprit being unspecified,@k only the 'process' leading to violence is the same.
This is one truth that emerges when you overlay the Fragments of different worlds.
This is one of the truths governing the infinite parallel worlds of the stage called Hinamizawa.
Namely, on this stage there exists a Rule X that states: 'an unspecified, random individual is seized by paranoid suspicion and lured toward violence.'
Keiichi Maebara,@k Shion Sonozaki,@k and Rena Ryuugu --@k pieces with no apparent connection -- each face the possibility of being consumed by that rule at random probability.
I don't know what probability governs which pieces are consumed by this rule.
There may be some underlying logic to it.
Perhaps the key is that all three are close in age,@k or perhaps it's that they all live in Hinamizawa.
...But that is something beyond the reach of us 'Rika Furudes' -- mere girls, neither detectives nor police.
Still, we've managed to expose the fact that such a mysterious Rule X exists on this stage called Hinamizawa.
This is something humans who inhabit a single world could never possibly comprehend.
I feel a certain delight in being able to understand it...
changeReason: Original says 'proud of myself'; JPN 愉悦 means 'delight/pleasure,' not pride; new correctly translates as 'I feel a certain delight.'

TextENGNew: ...Though for you, born into this world, it's probably so obvious you don't think much of it.
Hmm?@k What?
That's right.@k The more something recurs across worlds, the more it reflects a strong, deliberate will behind it.
changeReason: Original says 'fated to happen'; new more precisely captures JPN 強い意志を持って行なわれている as 'reflects a strong, deliberate will behind it,' distinguishing will from fate.

TextENGNew: Oh, hee hee.@k That would be the first thing you'd notice, wouldn't it?
...Yes. Jirou Tomitake and Miyo Takano are killed in these worlds with near-absolute certainty.
Moreover, the manner of their deaths is identical across every world.
...That is to say, unlike the instability of the rule we found earlier, their murders are carried out thoroughly from start to finish by a strong, unyielding will.
This does not fit the pattern of the mysterious, vague Rule X that lures an unspecified individual to violence.
After all, they are always killed regardless of who falls prey to that rule.
This tells us there exists a second, distinct rule -- Rule Y -- that dictates Jirou Tomitake and Miyo Takano will be killed on the night of Watanagashi every year.
Hee hee...@k Do you notice a certain dark comedy here?
In 'Onikakushi,' 'Watanagashi,' and 'Tsumihoroboshi' -- in every case -- Jirou Tomitake and the others are killed, right?
And in every one of those worlds, the character seized by the tragedy twists their deaths into something they believe is connected to the danger closing in on them -- don't they?

The rule by which someone is driven to violence -- Rule X --@k and the rule by which Tomitake and Takano are killed -- Rule Y -- should be two independent things,@k yet they all insist on linking the two together.
Of course, one could argue this is because the process of being lured toward violence involves being consumed by paranoid delusions to begin with...
But have you noticed that there is someone deliberately guiding the paranoid victims to link these two rules together?
Yes.@k The existence of this guiding piece is what makes the stage of Hinamizawa so convoluted.
You should have identified at least two of them.@k Do you know who they are?
That's right.
The pieces known as Kuraudo Ooishi and Miyo Takano.
The role of the piece called Kuraudo Ooishi becomes clear when you overlay the Fragments we have.
He's a detective nearing retirement, desperate to solve the Hinamizawa serial mysterious deaths before his time runs out.
Because of family circumstances, he'll have to move away once he retires.
So he is determined to solve the case at all costs while he still lives close enough to Hinamizawa.
Why is he so fixated on the serial mysterious deaths of Hinamizawa?
That's right.@k It's because of the very first incident -- the murder of the construction foreman.
The victim was a close personal friend of Kuraudo Ooishi.
This becomes clear if you examine the 'Himatsubushi' chapter's Fragment.
Ooishi was also present during the height of the dam conflict.
Having witnessed firsthand how the Sonozaki main family spearheaded the Onigafuchi Guardians, he became convinced they were the masterminds behind everything.
And so, regardless of whatever evidence did or did not exist, he had already decided from the start that the Sonozaki main family was the culprit behind the serial deaths.
His obsessive personal theory, lent credibility by his authority as a police detective, went on to influence the pitiable paranoia victims in each world.
As a result, the unfortunate 'victims' ensnared by Rule X found a convenient answer for their nameless paranoia -- the sinister machinations of the Sonozaki family.
...Perhaps the most conspicuous example was Shion Sonozaki's case.
Indeed -- if the piece called Ooishi hadn't meddled as he did, the victims of the rule might never have resorted to such extreme violence.
His is a piece whose role is to indirectly trigger tragedy.
Thinking about it that way, I don't know who coined it, but the title 'Oyashiro-sama's servant' bestowed upon Kuraudo Ooishi isn't so far off the mark.
changeReason: Original says it's the 'perfect nickname'; JPN そんなに的外れではない means 'not so far off the mark,' a more measured assessment that the new preserves.

TextENGNew: The other piece -- Miyo Takano -- plays an identical role.
changeReason: Original introduces Takano as 'suspicious person'; new correctly translates まったく同じ as 'plays an identical role,' which is the analytical framework being used.

TextENGNew: Miyo Takano's role, one would assume, is simply to amuse herself by researching Onigafuchi's eerie history and taking pleasure in frightening others with her findings.
However, in synergy with the piece called Ooishi, she plants in the pitiable rule-victims the paranoid conviction of a sinister, village-wide conspiracy led by the Sonozaki family.
The case where Miyo Takano intervened most excessively was with Rena Ryuugu.
For Miyo Takano, Rena Ryuugu -- consumed by paranoia and desperately grasping at any explanation -- must have been the perfect prey.
Miyo Takano, by her own inescapable fate, ends up killed in the end.
Had she survived and witnessed Rena's school siege, she would surely have been beside herself with laughter.
Now -- have you noticed that the pieces I just described, and the roles they come to fulfill, also follow their own rule?
The fact that they speak as they do, and that whoever listens to them believes what they say -- the fertile ground for that acceptance.
changeReason: Original truncates to 'they talk and listeners believe them'; new correctly captures the JPN about the 'fertile ground' (土壌) that enables both the talking and the believing.

TextENGNew: That is the darkly comedic third rule -- Rule Z.
The Fragments at hand should already give you a complete answer to this one.
Namely, the Sonozaki main family's tradition of bluffing -- behaving as though they are the masterminds behind everything, no matter what occurs.
The Sonozaki main family has 'pretended' to be the puppeteers behind every event that served their interests, all to maintain the illusion that they pull all the strings.
As a result, the villagers have ceased to question the assumption that the Sonozaki family has been maneuvering in the shadows ever since the dam conflict.
And the pieces that fell prey to this rule are none other than Kuraudo Ooishi and Miyo Takano.
In a broader sense, you could say the entire village is caught in its grip.
When you think about it this way,
changeReason: Original 'the more I think about it' misses JPN こうして考えると which means 'when you think about it this way' (referencing the analytical framework); new correctly links to the preceding analysis.

TextENGNew: the 'Hinamizawa Serial Mysterious Deaths' themselves become rather suspect.
changeReason: Original 'the stranger it seems' reverses the JPN meaning; new correctly renders it as the serial deaths 'themselves become rather suspect' - questioning whether they're really serial.

TextENGNew: Hee hee hee...@k Do you see why?
Nearly all the memory Fragments here are from 1983,@k so I know nothing about the incidents from 1979 through 1982.
But the very act of interpreting those incidents as 'serial'@k may itself be a consequence of falling victim to Rule Z -- the belief that the Sonozaki family is behind everything.
In other words, they may be forcing multiple incidents with entirely different causes into a single narrative.
Well, I have no means of verifying the truth from the Fragments available here.
Still, wasn't it mentioned somewhere in one of these Fragments?
That each of those incidents had been individually resolved.
Whether that's a sound argument or a reckless one, I can't say -- but it may turn out to be a truth we can't afford to dismiss.
Come to think of it, Shion said something fascinating in the 'Meakashi' chapter.
That 'curse system' she theorized.@k Do you remember?

Her hypothesis was that in Hinamizawa, there exists a tacit understanding that on the night of Watanagashi alone, it is permissible to kill the village's enemies in the name of Oyashiro-sama's curse --@k and that this foundation of 'murder is sanctioned on this one night' is the true identity of the curse.
Since most of the Fragments here are from 1983, we can't confirm Shion's hypothesis, but there are still intriguing elements to it.
Specifically, the notion that it isn't an individual criminal committing these crimes,@k but the 'environment' that is producing them.
People in a single world tend to analyze a crime by examining the perpetrator's history and the process leading to the act, but this idea takes a different approach.
In essence, it's the same as saying that the 'environment' that produced the robber is the true evil.
Robber A may have simply happened to be the one who committed the crime,@k but the environment of poverty might just as easily have produced Robber B, or even Robber C.
In another world, A might not be a criminal at all.
Therefore, the true culprit is not A, but the environment capable of producing A through C.

Well, people in a single world can arrive at this idea too,@k but their prevailing scapegoat mentality means they typically lynch A socially while turning a blind eye to the 'environment' that created A.
Oh... did I confuse you?
I'm sorry.
Perhaps that analogy was actually harder for you to grasp.
Since I was born in the ordinary world, it comes more naturally to me.

Let me put it in terms you'd understand.@k The culprit behind the tragedies across many worlds is not 'individuals' like Keiichi Maebara or Shion Sonozaki, but the 'environment' --@k meaning 'Rule X' itself is the true culprit.
If you can grasp that, then even without knowing the true identity of 'Rule X,' I'd say you've earned very high marks.
And if you've grasped the existence of the three invisible laws governing this stage called Hinamizawa -- Rules X, Y, and Z --
then you've earned even higher marks.
Once you understand these laws, even in a world you're experiencing for the first time, you should be able to find the truth without being led astray.
See?@k It's a fascinating world, isn't it?
Just three simple rules interact with one another, and upon this single stage called Hinamizawa, they produce worlds as varied as the patterns of a kaleidoscope.
I suspect this is a very rare case.
In other places, no matter how many times you repeated and overlaid worlds, you'd never see such extreme variation.
That's why I can sit here watching these worlds turn without growing bored -- for now, at least.
...Now, there is one final fact that emerges from overlaying these world Fragments.
And it is the single greatest problem plaguing us 'Rika Furudes.'
The fact that Rika Furude is killed -- every single time, without exception.
It's as though June 1983 has been sealed off into a dead end.
And unlike the random elements, this happens with absolute certainty.
Which means it follows the same law as the inevitable murders of Jirou Tomitake and the others --@k Rule Y.
Jirou Tomitake and Miyo Takano are killed on the night of Watanagashi with absolute certainty.
Rika Furude's date of death varies slightly, but she is almost always killed within the month of June.
changeReason: Original says 'dead by the middle of June'; JPN says ６月中に (within June), not by mid-June; new correctly translates as 'within the month of June.'

TextENGNew: Tomitake and Takano are presumably ambushed on their way home from the festival, which would explain the lack of variation in their date of death.
But Rika Furude is different.
In every world, her daily life and circumstances differ, and her behavioral patterns are unpredictable.
That is likely why the precise date of her murder varies.
However, the intent to kill her within June --@k the absolute determination to kill her -- carries the same powerful will as theirs.
So it stands to reason that the law by which Rika Furude is invariably killed operates under the same Rule Y as the murders of Tomitake and the others.
Then, is the culprit killing Rika Furude the same person who kills Jirou Tomitake and Miyo Takano...?
I can't say whether it's the same person, but the probability of it being the same rule at work is quite high.
The same person,@k or the same organization.
The same ideology,@k or the same objective.
For Rika, the paramount objective is to evade the murder targeting her.
That doesn't mean simply fleeing from the killer's grasp.
She must be able to continue living peacefully in the village afterward.
And every one of her friends must also remain safe and well.
That is Rika's happiness --@k the ideal future she hopes for.
...The fact that even such a modest wish cannot be realized no matter how many times she repeats this -- it can only be called a tragedy.
Though, of course, this is a tragedy that can only be perceived by beings like us -- those capable of overlaying and peering through the Fragments of different worlds.
Now, here is where an intriguing case presents itself.
Rika is killed by Rule Y in almost every case.
But have you noticed that in certain worlds, she is killed by Rule X instead?
Exactly.@k In the 'Watanagashi' and 'Meakashi' chapters.
In those worlds, Rika Furude did commit suicide, but we can say she was effectively killed by Rule X through Shion Sonozaki.
Which means... this would be a miscalculation for Rule Y.
Rule Y is targeting Rika with a strong, purposeful will, intent on killing her.
Yet she was eliminated by the entirely unrelated Rule X before Rule Y could act.
There may have been some kind of 'disturbance' on their end.
If Rule Y's objective is merely to eliminate Rika, then it's no problem.
But if there's some purpose beyond simply killing her...@k then that disruption might have manifested as some observable phenomenon.
In other words, there may be something common to both 'Watanagashi' and 'Meakashi'@k that does not occur in the other worlds.
Unfortunately, since we are Rika Furude, we don't know what happens after Rika dies.
...Or, perhaps......
Do you know?
Because I am Rika Furude, I only know what happens during her lifetime.
But you are a mysterious being born in this higher world that transcends the parallel ones.
Is there perhaps something that didn't happen in 'Watanagashi' and 'Meakashi'...
but did happen in the other worlds after Rika's death -- something you're aware of?
If you know anything... then that would surely be another truth behind Rule Y.
The Fragments here are all focused on the flashy turmoil stirred up by Rule X...
...but the foundation that sustains it -- Rule Z --@k and the undeniable 'malice' common to all worlds -- Rule Y --@k are what I believe truly govern this stage called Hinamizawa.
If that's the case, then Rika's path ahead is impossibly thorny.
To survive, she must fight Rule Y.
Unless she shatters it, Rika Furude has no future beyond June 1983.
And to claim her own happiness, she must wrest her friends' safety from Rule X.
Unless she overcomes it, one of Rika's precious friends may be lured into tragedy.
If that happens, even should Rika survive June 1983, it would not be the happy future she desires.
And she may also need to shatter Rule Z, just as Shion Sonozaki confronted it in 'Meakashi.'
So long as Rule Z persists, even if she escapes the 1983 tragedy, another may lie in wait in 1984 and beyond.
...It is extremely likely that Rule Z serves as the breeding ground for Rule X.
The deeply rooted assumption that the Sonozaki family lurks behind everything, and the custom of twisting any event into a manifestation of the curse -- this may indirectly be the root of all evil.
The fact that no one finds it strange when someone dies on the night of Watanagashi is perhaps the most telling example.
This Rule Z will remain like a curse upon the stage of Hinamizawa forever, serving as fertile soil for tragedy after tragedy.
Rules X, Y, and Z.
Three locks that imprison Rika Furude forever in June 1983.
changeReason: Original says 'three rules that hunt Rika down'; JPN 幽閉する means 'imprison/confine'; new correctly translates as 'three locks that imprison Rika Furude forever.'

TextENGNew: Each one is impregnable.
At first, she couldn't even tell that three locks existed.
changeReason: Original says 'we didn't figure out'; new correctly says she (Rika) 'couldn't even tell that three locks existed,' matching JPN 錠前が３つ存在することすらわからなかった with correct subject.

TextENGNew: Could you tell?
If you could, I'd give you top marks.
In the past, she challenged them time and again, only to be defeated each time.
changeReason: Original says 'I've tried so hard and was never successful'; new correctly uses third person and adds 'defeated each time' matching JPN 何度も挑んで敗北した.

TextENGNew: But in the 'Tsumihoroboshi' chapter, a miracle occurred.
Keiichi Maebara, by sheer chance, was able to perceive a faint Fragment from another world -- and he shattered Rule X.
...In the end, Rika was still killed that very night by Rule Y.
But one lock had been broken.
That was an unprecedented feat.

In the past, Rika had realized that three locks existed, but no matter what she tried, she could never break any of them.@k Eventually, resignation took hold, and she gradually lost interest in the worlds.
...And that is how I was born.
But the Rika of now is different.
She was taught something by the miracle of Keiichi Maebara -- a being who should have known only a single world.
changeReason: Original truncates to 'learned something valuable'; new preserves the full JPN meaning about learning from the miracle of someone who should have known only a single world.

TextENGNew: That with the will to fight, the locks can be broken.
If she can shatter all three locks before the end of June 1983,@k ...Rika Furude will be freed from her eternal prison of fate.
But the time remaining is truly not long.
Rika has journeyed through world after world after world.
Her physical body resets to youth each time, never aging -- but the mind does not enjoy the same luxury.
Mentally, she has aged beyond anything possible in a single world... and she is gradually being reborn into a being of a higher plane.
In the end, she will probably lose the ability to perceive any single world.
The signs are already there. Eventually, her memories of parallel worlds will blur together, and in the end she will descend into madness, losing the identity that is Rika Furude.
When that happens, Rika Furude will simply... vanish.
Which means this pitch-dark world will be left to just you and me.

The Fragments of memory -- our toys -- will gradually fade and disappear... and once true darkness has enveloped everything, in the eternity that follows, we will forget even each other's existence and meet our true end.
Rika Furude is dimly aware of this.
That it is not her body's lifespan, but her mind's, that is nearly spent.
And Hanyuu's power, too, is nearly exhausted.
She can no longer rewind years of time as she once could.
Her power has been steadily fading, and recently she can barely manage to rewind a single month.
No -- her power has already weakened to mere weeks.
The time allotted this next round will likely be less than two weeks.
A story in which Rika Furude will attempt to shatter all three locks -- in a mere two weeks.
